chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing electrolyte derangements in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge, focusing on feeding errors, diuretic effects, and monitoring protocols to prevent complications like growth failure and dehydration.
learning_objectives: Understand the causes and risks of electrolyte derangements in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge.
Learn the stepwise approach to assess and correct electrolyte abnormalities, including feeding and hydration evaluation.
Recognize the importance of timely electrolyte monitoring and growth tracking in high-risk infants.
Identify appropriate interventions for specific conditions like hyponatremia, hypernatremia, and hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis.
key_facts: Electrolyte derangements can result from concentrated feeds, reduced free water, or diuretic use.
Chronic hypochloremia and hyponatremia can impair growth and respiratory status in preterm infants.
For hypernatremia, stop unsafe feed concentration, provide free water, and escalate care if needed.
Electrolytes should be rechecked 1–2 weeks after any adjustment, or sooner in high-risk cases.
Tier 3 infants require an early electrolyte check at 2–4 weeks post-discharge, especially with diuretics or feed changes.
reference_quote: Electrolyte derangements are a predictable complication when feeds are concentrated, when total free water is reduced, or when loop/thiazide diuretics are continued after discharge.
document_key_topics: Impact of feeding errors and diuretics on electrolytes
Monitoring and intervention protocols for electrolyte abnormalities
